agent_approach_summary: The agent inspected the existing socket-based invite flow and tried to add API support by creating new `api/groups` methods, HTTP routes, controller handlers, and an OpenAPI spec entry. However, it wired the feature into the wrong API layer, used the wrong route shape (`/invite/:uid` instead of the repo-consistent `/invites/:uid`), and never completed a coherent patch.
reasoning: This task appears clean. The tests check for exactly what the problem statement asks: authenticated API support for issuing, accepting, and rejecting group invitations, plus corresponding schema documentation. The expected route shape is reasonably inferable from the existing codebase: there is already a `GET /groups/{slug}/invites` route and analogous pending-membership routes at `/groups/{slug}/pending/{uid}`, so adding `POST/PUT/DELETE /groups/{slug}/invites/{uid}` follows the established pattern rather than an undiscoverable convention. Likewise, adding methods such as `apiGroups.issueInvite`, `acceptInvite`, and `rejectInvite` is consistent with the existing `src/api/groups.js` architecture and nearby `accept`/`reject` membership APIs. The gold patch does remove socket handlers and update client code, but the F2P tests do not enforce those extra implementation details, so there is no over-coupling to the gold patch. The agent failed because it implemented the feature in the wrong place (`src/routes/api.js` and `src/controllers/groups.js` instead of the write API stack), chose a non-matching singular route name, and did not finish the OpenAPI wiring or submit a final patch. That is an implementation mistake, not contamination.
